title: Capstone Project
description: Choose one of four tracks to demonstrate mastery. Extend a prior project or build something new. Includes implementation, analysis report, and presentation. This is the culmination of your work in CS 101: you will spend three weeks on a substantial engineering effort that pushes one domain from the course to its limits. The result should be a system you are proud to present to a faculty committee and defend under questioning.

submissionRequirements.codeRepository: Complete Python codebase with clean structure, docstrings, and a README explaining how to run all components
submissionRequirements.analysisReport: 4-6 page technical report (PDF or Markdown) with architecture description, quantitative results, performance analysis with graphs, and discussion of tradeoffs and limitations
submissionRequirements.presentation: 12-15 minute presentation with live demo, covering motivation, architecture, key results, and lessons learned. Be prepared for 5 minutes of Q&A from the evaluation committee
submissionRequirements.weeklyCheckpoints: Submit milestone deliverables at the end of Weeks 18 and 19. These are graded pass/fail and serve as progress gates

tracks.title: Advanced RISC-V Processor
tracks.subtitle: Out-of-Order Superscalar Execution Engine
tracks.description: Transform your in-order pipelined RISC-V processor into a modern out-of-order superscalar machine. You will implement Tomasulo's algorithm with reservation stations, add a reorder buffer for speculative execution with precise exceptions, and extend to 2-wide superscalar issue. The final deliverable is a cycle-accurate simulator that demonstrates measurable IPC improvement over baseline in-order execution on benchmark programs.

required: Implement Tomasulo's algorithm with at least 6 reservation stations (2 for integer ALU, 2 for memory, 1 for multiply/divide, 1 for branch)
Implement a Common Data Bus (CDB) that broadcasts results to all waiting reservation stations and the ROB simultaneously
Implement register renaming via tags: the register alias table (RAT) maps architectural registers to reservation station or ROB entries, eliminating WAR and WAW hazards
Implement a Reorder Buffer (ROB) with at least 16 entries supporting in-order commit of out-of-order completions
Implement speculative execution: instructions after a branch are issued speculatively, and mispredictions trigger ROB rollback that restores the RAT and flushes speculative entries
Integrate your branch predictor from Project 2 (or implement a 2-bit saturating counter predictor at minimum) to drive speculative issue
Implement 2-wide superscalar issue: the processor fetches and issues up to 2 instructions per cycle, with dependency checking and resource arbitration to prevent structural hazards
Implement a comprehensive instruction trace log that shows cycle-by-cycle state of all reservation stations, the ROB, and the RAT
Run at least 3 benchmark programs (sorting, matrix multiply, recursive fibonacci or similar) and measure IPC for in-order, OoO, and OoO+superscalar configurations
Produce a performance analysis comparing the three configurations with IPC, total cycles, and CPI breakdown (data stalls, control stalls, structural stalls)
optional: Implement a load/store queue with memory disambiguation for out-of-order memory operations
Add a 3-wide or 4-wide issue mode and analyze diminishing returns
Implement a tournament branch predictor combining local and global history
Add performance counters: branch misprediction rate, reservation station utilization, ROB occupancy histogram

milestones.title: Tomasulo's Algorithm with Reservation Stations
deliverables: Working reservation station data structures with issue, execute, and write-back stages
Common Data Bus broadcasting to all waiting stations
Register Alias Table (RAT) correctly mapping architectural to physical tags
Cycle-by-cycle trace showing correct execution of a 10+ instruction sequence with RAW dependencies resolved via forwarding through the CDB
Demonstration that WAR and WAW hazards are eliminated by renaming
milestones.passFailCriteria: A test program with at least 3 RAW, 1 WAR, and 1 WAW hazard executes correctly with verifiable CDB broadcasts

milestones.title: Reorder Buffer and Speculative Execution
deliverables: ROB with in-order commit, supporting precise exceptions
Speculative issue of instructions past unresolved branches
Correct rollback on branch misprediction: ROB flush, RAT restoration, reservation station cleanup
Branch predictor integration with misprediction recovery
Test case demonstrating correct speculative execution followed by misprediction and clean recovery
milestones.passFailCriteria: A program with a branch misprediction correctly rolls back speculative state and resumes execution from the correct path with no corruption

milestones.title: Superscalar Issue and Performance Analysis
deliverables: 2-wide issue logic with dependency checking across the issue pair
Resource arbitration when both instructions target the same functional unit
Benchmark suite (3+ programs) with IPC measurements
Performance comparison report: in-order vs OoO vs OoO+superscalar
Analysis of bottlenecks and diminishing returns
Final presentation with live demo of cycle-by-cycle trace
milestones.passFailCriteria: Superscalar mode demonstrates measurable IPC > 1.0 on at least one benchmark

evaluationCriteria.design: Correct decomposition of Tomasulo's algorithm into clean data structures (reservation stations, ROB, RAT, CDB). Clear separation between issue, execute, write-back, and commit stages. Extensibility of the design to wider issue or more functional units.
evaluationCriteria.implementation: Cycle-accurate simulation with no state corruption. Correct CDB broadcast semantics. Proper speculative state management and rollback. No silent data corruption on misprediction recovery.
evaluationCriteria.analysis: Rigorous IPC comparison with explanation of observed speedups. Breakdown of stall causes. Discussion of what limits further IPC improvement (branch mispredictions, memory latency, limited functional units). Roofline-style analysis of achievable IPC vs. theoretical maximum.
evaluationCriteria.presentation: Live demonstration of cycle-by-cycle trace showing Tomasulo's in action. Clear explanation of the RAT, CDB, and ROB interaction. Ability to answer questions about edge cases (e.g., what happens when the ROB is full, when two CDB broadcasts collide).

tracks.starterCode:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum, auto
from typing import Optional
import json


class FunctionalUnit(Enum):
    INT_ALU = auto()
    INT_ALU_2 = auto()
    MEM_LOAD = auto()
    MEM_STORE = auto()
    MUL_DIV = auto()
    BRANCH = auto()


class RSStatus(Enum):
    EMPTY = auto()
    WAITING = auto()   # Waiting for operand(s) via CDB
    READY = auto()     # All operands available, waiting for FU
    EXECUTING = auto() # Currently in functional unit


@dataclass
class ReservationStation:
    """A single reservation station entry."""
    name: str                              # e.g., 'ALU1', 'MEM2'
    fu_type: FunctionalUnit
    status: RSStatus = RSStatus.EMPTY
    op: Optional[str] = None               # Instruction opcode
    vj: Optional[int] = None               # Value of source operand 1
    vk: Optional[int] = None               # Value of source operand 2
    qj: Optional[str] = None               # Tag of RS producing Vj (None if Vj ready)
    qk: Optional[str] = None               # Tag of RS producing Vk (None if Vk ready)
    dest: Optional[int] = None             # ROB entry index for destination
    address: Optional[int] = None          # Computed address for loads/stores
    cycles_remaining: int = 0              # Execution latency countdown

    def is_ready(self) -> bool:
        return self.qj is None and self.qk is None and self.status == RSStatus.WAITING

    def capture_cdb(self, tag: str, value: int):
        """Snoop the CDB: if we are waiting on this tag, capture the value."""
        if self.qj == tag:
            self.vj = value
            self.qj = None
        if self.qk == tag:
            self.vk = value
            self.qk = None


@dataclass
class ROBEntry:
    """A single Reorder Buffer entry."""
    index: int
    busy: bool = False
    instruction: Optional[str] = None      # Instruction string for debugging
    state: str = 'issue'                   # issue -> execute -> write -> commit
    dest_reg: Optional[int] = None         # Architectural destination register
    value: Optional[int] = None            # Computed result (filled at write-back)
    ready: bool = False                    # True when value is available
    is_branch: bool = False
    branch_predicted_taken: bool = False
    branch_actual_taken: Optional[bool] = None
    speculative: bool = False              # True if issued under speculation
    pc: int = 0                            # Program counter of this instruction


@dataclass
class CDBBroadcast:
    """A Common Data Bus broadcast message."""
    tag: str              # Source reservation station or ROB index
    value: int            # The computed result
    rob_index: int        # Which ROB entry this completes


class RegisterAliasTable:
    """Maps architectural registers to the ROB entry that will produce their value."""

    def __init__(self, num_regs: int = 32):
        # None means the value is in the register file (not being produced by an in-flight instruction)
        self.table: list[Optional[int]] = [None] * num_regs

    def set_mapping(self, arch_reg: int, rob_index: int):
        """Record that arch_reg will be produced by rob_index."""
        if arch_reg == 0:  # x0 is always zero in RISC-V
            return
        self.table[arch_reg] = rob_index

    def get_mapping(self, arch_reg: int) -> Optional[int]:
        """Return the ROB index producing this register, or None if it's ready in the RF."""
        if arch_reg == 0:
            return None
        return self.table[arch_reg]

    def clear_mapping(self, arch_reg: int, rob_index: int):
        """Clear the mapping only if it still points to this ROB entry.
        (A later instruction may have overwritten it.)"""
        if arch_reg != 0 and self.table[arch_reg] == rob_index:
            self.table[arch_reg] = None

    def snapshot(self) -> list[Optional[int]]:
        """Return a copy for rollback on misprediction."""
        return self.table.copy()

    def restore(self, snapshot: list[Optional[int]]):
        """Restore from a snapshot after branch misprediction."""
        self.table = snapshot


class OoOProcessor:
    """Out-of-order RISC-V processor simulator using Tomasulo's algorithm."""

    def __init__(self, program: list[dict], memory_size: int = 4096):
        self.program = program           # List of decoded instructions
        self.pc = 0
        self.cycle = 0
        self.halted = False

        # Architectural state
        self.register_file = [0] * 32    # x0-x31
        self.memory = [0] * memory_size

        # Tomasulo structures
        self.reservation_stations = self._init_reservation_stations()
        self.rob = [ROBEntry(index=i) for i in range(16)]
        self.rob_head = 0                # Oldest instruction (commit pointer)
        self.rob_tail = 0                # Next free entry (issue pointer)
        self.rat = RegisterAliasTable()
        self.rat_snapshots: dict[int, list[Optional[int]]] = {}  # ROB index -> RAT snapshot for branches

        # Superscalar config
        self.issue_width = 1             # Set to 2 for superscalar

        # Statistics
        self.instructions_committed = 0
        self.branch_predictions = 0
        self.branch_mispredictions = 0
        self.cycles_stalled_structural = 0
        self.trace_log: list[dict] = []

    def _init_reservation_stations(self) -> list[ReservationStation]:
        """Create the reservation stations for each functional unit type."""
        stations = [
            ReservationStation('ALU1', FunctionalUnit.INT_ALU),
            ReservationStation('ALU2', FunctionalUnit.INT_ALU_2),
            ReservationStation('MEM1', FunctionalUnit.MEM_LOAD),
            ReservationStation('MEM2', FunctionalUnit.MEM_STORE),
            ReservationStation('MUL1', FunctionalUnit.MUL_DIV),
            ReservationStation('BR1', FunctionalUnit.BRANCH),
        ]
        return stations

    def step(self):
        """Execute one clock cycle: commit -> write-back -> execute -> issue (reverse order for correctness)."""
        # TODO: Implement the four stages in reverse pipeline order
        # 1. Commit: retire instructions from ROB head if ready
        # 2. Write-back: broadcast completed results on CDB
        # 3. Execute: advance executing instructions, start ready ones
        # 4. Issue: fetch next instruction(s) and allocate RS + ROB entries
        self.cycle += 1
        self._record_trace()

    def _commit(self):
        """Commit the instruction at ROB head if it has completed.
        For branches, check prediction and trigger rollback if mispredicted."""
        raise NotImplementedError

    def _write_back(self) -> list[CDBBroadcast]:
        """Broadcast completed results on the CDB.
        Each cycle, at most one broadcast (or two for superscalar with dual CDB)."""
        raise NotImplementedError

    def _execute(self):
        """Decrement cycle counters for executing instructions.
        Start execution for ready reservation stations."""
        raise NotImplementedError

    def _issue(self):
        """Issue up to self.issue_width instructions per cycle.
        For each: allocate RS, allocate ROB entry, read/rename operands via RAT."""
        raise NotImplementedError

    def _rollback(self, rob_index: int):
        """Handle branch misprediction: flush all ROB entries after rob_index,
        free their reservation stations, restore RAT snapshot."""
        raise NotImplementedError

    def _record_trace(self):
        """Record current cycle state for debugging and analysis."""
        state = {
            'cycle': self.cycle,
            'pc': self.pc,
            'rob_head': self.rob_head,
            'rob_tail': self.rob_tail,
            'rs_status': {rs.name: rs.status.name for rs in self.reservation_stations},
            'rat': self.rat.table.copy(),
        }
        self.trace_log.append(state)

    def get_ipc(self) -> float:
        """Instructions per cycle = committed instructions / total cycles."""
        if self.cycle == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.instructions_committed / self.cycle

    def run(self, max_cycles: int = 10000) -> dict:
        """Run until halt or max_cycles. Return performance statistics."""
        while not self.halted and self.cycle < max_cycles:
            self.step()
        return {
            'cycles': self.cycle,
            'instructions_committed': self.instructions_committed,
            'ipc': self.get_ipc(),
            'branch_misprediction_rate': (
                self.branch_mispredictions / max(1, self.branch_predictions)
            ),
            'cycles_stalled_structural': self.cycles_stalled_structural,
        }

tracks.title: GPU Performance Engineering
tracks.subtitle: Iterative Optimization of a Compute-Intensive Algorithm
tracks.description: Select a real-world compute-intensive algorithm -- Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), Sparse Matrix-Vector Multiply (SpMV), or N-body gravitational simulation -- and implement it on the GPU simulator from Project 4. Then systematically optimize through at least 5 iterations, measuring and documenting the performance impact of each optimization. The goal is not just a fast implementation, but a rigorous understanding of why each optimization works and when it hits diminishing returns.

algorithmChoices.name: FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)
algorithmChoices.description: Implement the Cooley-Tukey radix-2 FFT. Start with a naive per-butterfly kernel, then optimize memory access patterns, exploit shared memory for intra-block stages, and use warp-level shuffles for the final stages.
algorithmChoices.complexity: O(N log N) with N up to 2^20

algorithmChoices.name: SpMV (Sparse Matrix-Vector Multiply)
algorithmChoices.description: Implement y = Ax for sparse matrices in CSR format. Start with one-thread-per-row, then optimize for warp-level reduction, handle load imbalance across rows, and implement the SELL-C-sigma format for better coalescing.
algorithmChoices.complexity: O(nnz) with matrices up to 1M rows, 10M nonzeros

algorithmChoices.name: N-body Simulation
algorithmChoices.description: Implement gravitational N-body with direct all-pairs force calculation. Start with a naive O(N^2) kernel, optimize with shared memory tiling, software-managed cache of particle positions, and explore the Barnes-Hut tree approximation on GPU.
algorithmChoices.complexity: O(N^2) direct or O(N log N) tree-based, N up to 65536

required: Implement the chosen algorithm correctly on the GPU simulator with verification against a reference CPU implementation

Implement at least 5 distinct optimization levels, each building on the previous: (1) Naive baseline, (2) Coalesced memory access, (3) Shared memory tiling, (4) Warp-level optimization (warp shuffles, warp-level reduction), (5) Multiple elements per thread (thread coarsening)
For each optimization, measure and record: execution time (simulated cycles), memory transactions (global loads, shared loads), arithmetic intensity, and occupancy
Compute the speedup of each optimization relative to the naive baseline AND relative to the previous optimization level
Verify correctness after each optimization: results must match the naive baseline within floating-point tolerance (1e-6 relative error)
Construct a roofline model for the GPU simulator's architecture and plot each optimization level's achieved performance against the roofline ceiling
Identify the bottleneck at each optimization level: is the kernel compute-bound or memory-bound? What resource limits occupancy?
Write a performance analysis report explaining the optimization journey with quantitative data for every claim
Produce at least 3 plots: (a) speedup progression across optimizations, (b) roofline plot, (c) one additional plot of your choice (occupancy vs. performance, memory transaction breakdown, etc.)
optional: Implement a 6th optimization: mixed precision (FP16 accumulation with FP32 master copy) and analyze accuracy vs. performance tradeoff
Implement auto-tuning: sweep block sizes and elements-per-thread, find the Pareto frontier of performance vs. register usage
Compare your GPU implementation against a theoretical CPU implementation and compute the GPU advantage factor
Implement a second algorithm and compare the optimization profiles (which optimizations matter most for compute-bound vs. memory-bound kernels)

milestones.title: Baseline Implementation and Correctness Verification
deliverables: Reference CPU implementation of the chosen algorithm with test cases
Naive GPU kernel implementation (optimization level 0)
Correctness verification framework: automated comparison against CPU reference
Baseline performance measurement: cycles, memory transactions, arithmetic intensity
Roofline model constructed for the GPU simulator architecture
milestones.passFailCriteria: Naive GPU kernel produces correct results (within 1e-6 relative error of CPU reference) on 3 test inputs of increasing size

milestones.title: Optimizations 1-3 with Measured Impact
deliverables: Optimization 1: Coalesced memory access with measured reduction in global memory transactions
Optimization 2: Shared memory tiling with tile size analysis
Optimization 3: Warp-level optimization with measured reduction in synchronization overhead
Correctness verified for all three optimization levels
Speedup chart showing cumulative improvement
Per-optimization bottleneck analysis
milestones.passFailCriteria: All three optimized kernels pass correctness checks, and at least 2 show measurable speedup over baseline

milestones.title: Optimizations 4-5, Roofline Analysis, and Final Report
deliverables: Optimization 4: Multiple elements per thread with thread coarsening analysis
Optimization 5: Additional optimization specific to chosen algorithm
Roofline plot with all optimization levels annotated
Complete performance analysis report with all required plots
Discussion of diminishing returns and theoretical performance ceiling
Final presentation with live demo of optimization progression
milestones.passFailCriteria: Final optimized kernel achieves at least 5x speedup over naive baseline on the largest test input

evaluationCriteria.design: Clean separation between algorithm logic and GPU optimization. Modular kernel implementations that make it easy to compare optimization levels. Well-structured measurement harness that automates correctness checking and performance data collection.
evaluationCriteria.implementation: All optimization levels correct and measurably distinct. Proper use of GPU memory hierarchy (global, shared, registers). Correct synchronization. No race conditions or bank conflicts in shared memory implementations.
evaluationCriteria.analysis: Quantitative data for every performance claim. Roofline analysis that correctly identifies compute-bound vs. memory-bound regimes. Insightful explanation of why each optimization helps (or doesn't). Discussion of diminishing returns and what would be needed to go further.
evaluationCriteria.presentation: Clear progression narrative from naive to optimized. Ability to explain the roofline model and where each kernel sits. Demonstration of the measurement infrastructure. Ability to answer 'what if' questions about alternative optimization strategies.

tracks.title: FPGA Trading System
tracks.subtitle: Multi-Symbol Exchange with Backtesting and Pipeline Optimization
tracks.description: Extend your single-symbol FPGA trading system into a multi-symbol exchange simulator capable of trading 5 or more symbols simultaneously. Add a backtesting engine that replays historical market data and computes standard trading performance metrics. Optimize the processing pipeline for throughput and latency, demonstrating the kind of engineering that makes FPGA-based trading competitive in real markets.

required: Implement a multi-symbol order book manager that maintains independent order books for at least 5 symbols, with a feed demultiplexer that routes incoming market data to the correct book
Implement cross-symbol correlation signals: detect when price movements in one symbol predict movements in another (e.g., simple pairs trading signal based on spread deviation from historical mean)

Build a backtesting engine that replays a sequence of at least 10,000 historical market events (can be synthetically generated with realistic properties: bid-ask spread, order arrivals following Poisson process, price following geometric Brownian motion)
Compute and track standard trading metrics: total P&L, Sharpe ratio (annualized), maximum drawdown, win rate, profit factor (gross profit / gross loss), average trade duration
Implement pipeline stages for parallel symbol processing: the system should process events for different symbols concurrently, modeling the parallelism available in FPGA fabric
Measure and report throughput (events processed per simulated second) and worst-case latency (maximum time from event arrival to order decision)
Implement at least 2 trading strategies that can be backtested: (a) a momentum/trend-following strategy, (b) a mean-reversion/statistical arbitrage strategy
Produce a backtesting report comparing the two strategies across the same historical data, with equity curves, drawdown charts, and risk-adjusted metrics
Implement risk management: per-symbol position limits, portfolio-level exposure limits, and maximum loss circuit breakers
optional: Implement order book imbalance signals (bid/ask volume ratio) as a short-term predictor
Add latency modeling: different pipeline stages have different latencies, and the system must handle events arriving during processing
Implement a market-making strategy that quotes both sides and manages inventory risk
Add configurable FPGA resource constraints (number of pipeline stages, BRAM budget) and show how performance degrades as resources are reduced

milestones.title: Multi-Symbol Order Book and Feed Demultiplexer
deliverables: Multi-symbol order book data structure with independent books per symbol
Feed demultiplexer that routes market data events to the correct order book by symbol ID
Cross-symbol correlation tracker that maintains rolling statistics
Test suite verifying correct order book maintenance for 5 symbols under concurrent event streams
Event generator producing realistic synthetic market data for multiple symbols
milestones.passFailCriteria: 5-symbol order book correctly processes 1000 events with no cross-contamination between symbols, verified by post-hoc order book state audit

milestones.title: Backtesting Framework with Performance Metrics
deliverables: Backtesting engine that replays historical events and executes trading strategies
At least 2 trading strategies implemented and testable
Performance metrics computation: Sharpe ratio, max drawdown, P&L, win rate, profit factor
Equity curve and drawdown tracking over the full backtest period
Risk management enforcement: position limits, exposure limits, circuit breakers
milestones.passFailCriteria: Backtest of 10,000+ events completes for both strategies with all metrics computed correctly (verified against hand-calculated values for a small subsample)

milestones.title: Pipeline Optimization and Analysis Report
deliverables: Pipeline architecture for parallel multi-symbol processing
Throughput and latency measurements with and without pipelining
Strategy comparison report with equity curves and risk-adjusted metrics
Analysis of pipeline bottlenecks and resource utilization
Final presentation with live backtesting demo
milestones.passFailCriteria: Pipelined architecture demonstrates at least 3x throughput improvement over serial processing on 5-symbol workload

evaluationCriteria.design: Clean abstraction between market data handling, strategy logic, order management, and risk controls. Modular strategy interface that makes it easy to add new strategies. Pipeline architecture that reflects real FPGA design patterns (registered stages, data-flow style).
evaluationCriteria.implementation: Correct order book semantics (price-time priority, proper matching). Accurate backtest replay with no look-ahead bias. Correct metric calculations (especially Sharpe ratio annualization and drawdown tracking). Proper risk management enforcement.
evaluationCriteria.analysis: Meaningful strategy comparison with statistical discussion (is the Sharpe ratio significantly different? What is the confidence interval?). Throughput/latency analysis that identifies the bottleneck pipeline stage. Discussion of how the system would scale to 50 or 500 symbols.
evaluationCriteria.presentation: Live demo of backtesting with real-time equity curve. Clear explanation of the pipeline architecture. Ability to discuss strategy design choices and their market microstructure rationale.

tracks.starterCode:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum, auto
from typing import Optional
from collections import deque
import math
import json
import random


class Side(Enum):
    BID = auto()
    ASK = auto()


@dataclass
class Order:
    symbol: str
    side: Side
    price: int           # Fixed-point price (e.g., cents)
    quantity: int
    timestamp: int       # Nanosecond timestamp
    order_id: int = 0


@dataclass
class Trade:
    symbol: str
    price: int
    quantity: int
    aggressor_side: Side
    timestamp: int
    buyer_order_id: int
    seller_order_id: int


@dataclass
class MarketEvent:
    """A single market data event from the feed."""
    event_type: str      # 'new_order', 'cancel', 'trade', 'quote_update'
    symbol: str
    timestamp: int
    data: dict


@dataclass
class PerformanceMetrics:
    """Trading performance metrics computed over a backtest."""
    total_pnl: float = 0.0
    num_trades: int = 0
    num_winning_trades: int = 0
    num_losing_trades: int = 0
    gross_profit: float = 0.0
    gross_loss: float = 0.0
    max_drawdown: float = 0.0
    peak_equity: float = 0.0
    current_equity: float = 0.0
    equity_curve: list = field(default_factory=list)
    daily_returns: list = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def win_rate(self) -> float:
        if self.num_trades == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.num_winning_trades / self.num_trades

    @property
    def profit_factor(self) -> float:
        if self.gross_loss == 0:
            return float('inf') if self.gross_profit > 0 else 0.0
        return self.gross_profit / abs(self.gross_loss)

    @property
    def sharpe_ratio(self) -> float:
        """Annualized Sharpe ratio assuming 252 trading days."""
        if len(self.daily_returns) < 2:
            return 0.0
        mean_return = sum(self.daily_returns) / len(self.daily_returns)
        variance = sum((r - mean_return) ** 2 for r in self.daily_returns) / (len(self.daily_returns) - 1)
        std_return = math.sqrt(variance)
        if std_return == 0:
            return 0.0
        return (mean_return / std_return) * math.sqrt(252)

    def update_drawdown(self, equity: float):
        """Track peak equity and maximum drawdown."""
        self.current_equity = equity
        self.equity_curve.append(equity)
        if equity > self.peak_equity:
            self.peak_equity = equity
        drawdown = (self.peak_equity - equity) / self.peak_equity if self.peak_equity > 0 else 0.0
        if drawdown > self.max_drawdown:
            self.max_drawdown = drawdown


class OrderBook:
    """A limit order book for a single symbol."""

    def __init__(self, symbol: str):
        self.symbol = symbol
        self.bids: list[Order] = []   # Sorted by price desc, then time asc
        self.asks: list[Order] = []   # Sorted by price asc, then time asc
        self.trade_log: list[Trade] = []

    def add_order(self, order: Order) -> list[Trade]:
        """Add an order, matching against resting orders. Return any resulting trades."""
        # TODO: Implement price-time priority matching
        raise NotImplementedError

    def cancel_order(self, order_id: int) -> bool:
        """Cancel a resting order by ID. Return True if found and cancelled."""
        raise NotImplementedError

    @property
    def best_bid(self) -> Optional[int]:
        return self.bids[0].price if self.bids else None

    @property
    def best_ask(self) -> Optional[int]:
        return self.asks[0].price if self.asks else None

    @property
    def spread(self) -> Optional[int]:
        if self.best_bid is not None and self.best_ask is not None:
            return self.best_ask - self.best_bid
        return None


class MultiSymbolBook:
    """Manages order books for multiple symbols with feed demultiplexing."""

    def __init__(self, symbols: list[str]):
        self.books = {symbol: OrderBook(symbol) for symbol in symbols}
        self.event_count = 0

    def route_event(self, event: MarketEvent) -> Optional[list[Trade]]:
        """Route a market event to the appropriate order book."""
        if event.symbol not in self.books:
            return None
        self.event_count += 1
        # TODO: Dispatch to correct book based on event type
        raise NotImplementedError


class TradingStrategy:
    """Base class for trading strategies."""

    def __init__(self, name: str):
        self.name = name

    def on_market_event(self, event: MarketEvent, books: MultiSymbolBook) -> list[Order]:
        """Process a market event and return any orders to place."""
        raise NotImplementedError


class BacktestEngine:
    """Replay historical events through a strategy and compute metrics."""

    def __init__(self, symbols: list[str], strategy: TradingStrategy,
                 initial_capital: float = 1_000_000.0):
        self.books = MultiSymbolBook(symbols)
        self.strategy = strategy
        self.capital = initial_capital
        self.positions: dict[str, int] = {s: 0 for s in symbols}  # shares held
        self.metrics = PerformanceMetrics()
        self.metrics.peak_equity = initial_capital
        self.metrics.current_equity = initial_capital

    def replay(self, events: list[MarketEvent]) -> PerformanceMetrics:
        """Replay all events, executing the strategy for each."""
        for event in events:
            # 1. Update order book
            trades = self.books.route_event(event)
            # 2. Let strategy react
            orders = self.strategy.on_market_event(event, self.books)
            # 3. Execute strategy orders against the book
            for order in orders:
                strategy_trades = self.books.books[order.symbol].add_order(order)
                self._process_strategy_trades(strategy_trades)
            # 4. Update equity and metrics
            self._update_equity()
        return self.metrics

    def _process_strategy_trades(self, trades: list[Trade]):
        """Update positions and P&L from strategy trades."""
        # TODO: Update self.positions, self.capital, self.metrics
        raise NotImplementedError

    def _update_equity(self):
        """Compute total equity = capital + mark-to-market positions."""
        # TODO: Mark positions to current mid price
        raise NotImplementedError


def generate_synthetic_events(symbols: list[str], num_events: int,
                               seed: int = 42) -> list[MarketEvent]:
    """Generate synthetic market events with realistic properties.
    Prices follow geometric Brownian motion, arrivals are Poisson-distributed."""
    random.seed(seed)
    events = []
    prices = {s: 10000 for s in symbols}  # Start at $100.00 (fixed-point cents)
    timestamp = 0
    for _ in range(num_events):
        symbol = random.choice(symbols)
        # Price update: geometric Brownian motion step
        drift = 0.0001
        volatility = 0.002
        prices[symbol] = int(prices[symbol] * (1 + drift + volatility * random.gauss(0, 1)))
        # Inter-arrival time: exponential (Poisson process)
        timestamp += int(random.expovariate(1.0 / 1000))  # avg 1000 ns between events
        event = MarketEvent(
            event_type='quote_update',
            symbol=symbol,
            timestamp=timestamp,
            data={
                'bid_price': prices[symbol] - random.randint(1, 5),
                'ask_price': prices[symbol] + random.randint(1, 5),
                'bid_size': random.randint(100, 1000),
                'ask_size': random.randint(100, 1000),
            }
        )
        events.append(event)
    return events

tracks.title: Supercomputer Interconnect Simulator
tracks.subtitle: Topology Design, MPI Collectives, and Congestion Analysis
tracks.description: Build a simulator for a 1024-node supercomputer interconnect that models multiple network topologies, implements MPI collective communication algorithms, and analyzes bandwidth utilization and congestion patterns. This is an original project (not extending a prior one) that synthesizes concepts from weeks 11, 12, and 17. You will discover why topology choice is one of the most consequential decisions in supercomputer design, and why collective communication algorithms must be co-designed with the network.

required: Implement a configurable 1024-node system with 3 topology generators: (a) k-ary fat tree (at least 2-level with configurable radix), (b) dragonfly (groups of switches with local and global links), (c) 3D torus (with configurable dimensions, e.g., 8x8x16)
Each topology must correctly compute: number of switches, number of links, total bisection bandwidth, network diameter, and average path length

Implement routing algorithms: (a) shortest-path deterministic routing (Dijkstra or BFS), (b) adaptive routing that can route around congestion by choosing among multiple shortest paths or near-shortest paths

Implement a latency model: message_latency = alpha + beta * message_size + gamma * hops, where alpha (startup latency), beta (per-byte transfer time), and gamma (per-hop latency) are configurable parameters

Simulate 3 MPI collective operations: (a) Broadcast using a binomial tree algorithm, (b) Allreduce using the ring algorithm (Rabenseifner), (c) Alltoall using Bruck's algorithm or a simple direct-exchange

For each collective on each topology, measure: total completion time, per-link bandwidth utilization, maximum link utilization (bottleneck link), and message volume (total bytes transferred across all links)
Implement a congestion tracker that records per-link utilization over time and identifies bottleneck links where utilization exceeds a configurable threshold
Produce a topology comparison report: for each collective, compare completion time, bisection bandwidth utilization, and congestion across all 3 topologies
Analyze how performance scales with message size: sweep message sizes from 1 KB to 1 GB and plot completion time vs. message size for each topology-collective pair
optional: Implement a 4th topology: slim fly (based on graph theory for near-optimal diameter) and compare against the other three
Implement non-blocking Alltoall with overlapped computation and communication phases
Model link failures: randomly disable links and measure the impact on collective completion time with adaptive vs. deterministic routing
Implement hierarchical collectives that exploit topology structure (e.g., intra-group reduce followed by inter-group reduce for dragonfly)

milestones.title: Topology Generators and Routing Algorithms
deliverables: Fat tree, dragonfly, and 3D torus topology generators producing correct node/switch/link graphs
Topology metadata computation: diameter, bisection bandwidth, average path length, total links
Shortest-path deterministic routing with verified correctness (all nodes reachable, paths are shortest)
Adaptive routing with at least 2 alternative paths between any pair
Visualization or structured dump of topology graphs for verification
milestones.passFailCriteria: All 3 topologies correctly connect 1024 nodes, shortest-path routing reaches all node pairs, and computed diameter matches expected values for each topology

milestones.title: MPI Collective Simulators
deliverables: Broadcast (binomial tree) working on all 3 topologies with correct message delivery to all nodes
Allreduce (ring algorithm) producing correct reduced values at all nodes
Alltoall (Bruck's algorithm) delivering all point-to-point messages correctly
Latency model applied to all collectives with configurable alpha/beta/gamma
Per-link utilization tracking during collective execution
milestones.passFailCriteria: All 3 collectives produce correct results on all 3 topologies, verified by comparing against a reference serial implementation

milestones.title: Congestion Analysis and Topology Comparison Report
deliverables: Congestion tracker identifying bottleneck links per topology-collective pair
Message size sweep (1 KB to 1 GB) with completion time plots
Topology comparison matrix: 3 topologies x 3 collectives x multiple message sizes
Analysis of which topology is best for which collective pattern and why
Discussion of cost-performance tradeoffs (total links/switches vs. performance)
Final presentation with topology visualizations and performance comparison
milestones.passFailCriteria: Complete comparison matrix with at least 5 message sizes, identifying the best topology for each collective and explaining the result

evaluationCriteria.design: Clean topology abstraction (common interface for all topologies with topology-specific generators). Separation between topology, routing, collective algorithm, and measurement. Extensible design that would allow adding new topologies or collectives without modifying existing code.
evaluationCriteria.implementation: Correct topology construction (right number of nodes, switches, links, correct connectivity). Correct routing (all paths valid, shortest paths are actually shortest). Correct collective semantics (broadcast delivers to all, allreduce produces correct sums, alltoall completes all exchanges).
evaluationCriteria.analysis: Insightful comparison that goes beyond raw numbers. Explanation of why fat tree excels for all-to-all but dragonfly is more cost-effective for nearest-neighbor. Analysis of how congestion patterns differ across topologies. Discussion of real-world implications (cost per switch, cabling complexity, fault tolerance).
evaluationCriteria.presentation: Clear topology diagrams (even ASCII art). Compelling performance comparison with well-labeled charts. Ability to explain routing decisions and collective algorithms at the whiteboard level. Readiness to discuss how results would change with different node counts or radix choices.

criteria: Clean decomposition into well-defined classes with single responsibilities
Appropriate use of abstraction (base classes, interfaces) that enables extension without modification
Data structure choices that match the problem domain and support efficient operations
Separation of concerns between simulation logic, measurement, and reporting
Design decisions are documented and justified in the report

gradingScale.excellent: 45-50 points. Elegant architecture that a new team member could understand in 30 minutes. Abstractions are well-chosen and non-leaky. Extensibility is demonstrated, not just claimed.
gradingScale.good: 35-44 points. Sound architecture with minor issues. Most abstractions are appropriate. Some coupling between components that could be improved.
gradingScale.adequate: 25-34 points. Working architecture but with significant coupling or unclear abstractions. Design choices are not well justified.
gradingScale.poor: 0-24 points. Monolithic code, god classes, or inappropriate abstractions. No clear separation of concerns.

criteria: All required features are implemented and functional
Correctness is verified through tests or comparison against reference implementations
Edge cases are handled (empty inputs, boundary conditions, error states)
Code is readable: meaningful names, docstrings, type hints, consistent style
No silent failures or data corruption under valid inputs

gradingScale.excellent: 52-60 points. All required features work correctly. Edge cases handled. Clean, well-documented code. Tests cover critical paths.
gradingScale.good: 40-51 points. Most features work. Minor edge cases missed. Code is readable but could use more documentation.
gradingScale.adequate: 28-39 points. Core features work but some required features are incomplete. Some edge cases cause incorrect results.
gradingScale.poor: 0-27 points. Multiple features broken or missing. Frequent incorrect results. Code is difficult to follow.

analysis.description: Depth of analysis, data presentation, and insights
criteria: Every performance claim is backed by quantitative data
Appropriate metrics are chosen and correctly computed
Results are presented in well-labeled charts and tables
Analysis goes beyond stating numbers to explaining why results occur
Limitations and threats to validity are discussed honestly
Comparison against baselines or theoretical bounds where applicable

gradingScale.excellent: 45-50 points. Rigorous quantitative analysis with insightful explanations. Professional-quality charts. Honest discussion of limitations. Results are reproducible.
gradingScale.good: 35-44 points. Solid analysis with quantitative backing. Charts are clear. Some insights could be deeper.
gradingScale.adequate: 25-34 points. Analysis is present but shallow. Some claims lack data. Charts are hard to read or mislabeled.
gradingScale.poor: 0-24 points. Little or no quantitative analysis. Claims are unsupported. No charts or only trivial ones.

criteria: Presentation is well-structured with clear narrative arc (motivation -> approach -> results -> conclusions)
Live demo works reliably and showcases the system's capabilities
Slides/visuals are clear, uncluttered, and support the narrative
Speaker demonstrates deep understanding of the system, not just surface-level familiarity
Q&A responses are thoughtful, technically accurate, and acknowledge limitations when appropriate

gradingScale.excellent: 36-40 points. Compelling presentation with flawless demo. Speaker clearly understands every component. Q&A responses are precise and insightful.
gradingScale.good: 28-35 points. Clear presentation with working demo. Speaker handles most questions well.
gradingScale.adequate: 20-27 points. Presentation is understandable but unfocused. Demo has minor issues. Q&A responses are vague.
gradingScale.poor: 0-19 points. Presentation is disorganized or confusing. Demo fails. Speaker cannot answer basic questions about their own system.

academicIntegrityPolicy: This is an individual project. You may consult textbooks, papers, and online references, but all code must be your own. You must cite any external resources that influenced your design. Using AI code generators (Copilot, ChatGPT, etc.) for boilerplate is permitted, but you must understand and be able to explain every line of code you submit. During the Q&A portion of your presentation, the evaluators will probe your understanding -- inability to explain your own code will be treated as an academic integrity violation.

Harris, L. 'Trading and Exchanges: Market Microstructure for Practitioners'
